deconstruction_targets: legal fiction of relapse into heresy after abjuration
strong suspicion as basis for condemnation without proof
constructive_proposals: commanding the strongly suspected to abjure all heresy in general and particularly that which they are suspected of
handing over to secular court after a year of obstinate refusal following excommunication

scapegoat.name: the witch
attributes: commits temporal harms (injuries to men, cattle, and goods)
scapegoat.justification: The text constructs the witch as a figure whose actions — especially refusal to abjure and causing harm — justify both ecclesiastical condemnation and secular execution, despite weak or indirect evidence of heresy.

moral_justification: Punishment is justified by the combination of strong suspicion, refusal to recant, and demonstrable harm to community and property; the text asserts that moral corruption and physical damage override procedural leniency.
summary: The passage deconstructs canonical procedures for handling heresy suspicions, exposing contradictions in legal fictions and disproportionate punishments, while constructing a rigid, punitive framework where refusal to abjure and causing harm justify execution, particularly targeting witches with indirect or circumstantial evidence.
